input_path: data\papers\The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin (PT)** system under study is a **Controlled Plant System (CPS)** designed for temperature regulation in an **insulated incubator environment**. The system consists of:
  - A **styrofoam box** as an insulated container to minimize heat loss.
  - A **heatbed** as the primary heating element to maintain internal temperature.
  - A **fan** for uniform heat distribution within the box.
  - **Two internal temperature sensors** (for monitoring the internal temperature of the contents) and **one external temperature sensor** (to measure ambient temperature outside the styrofoam box).
  The operational domain focuses on **temperature regulation** within a controlled range, leveraging a **Raspberry Pi-based control system** to regulate the heatbed and fan based on sensor inputs. The system is designed for **reconfigurability** and **robustness** against varying environmental conditions, making it suitable for applications requiring precise and adaptive thermal management (e.g., incubators for biological samples). The hardware setup is documented in the publicly available repository [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator), which includes detailed specifications for hardware and software components.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The Digital Twin (DT) for this incubator system provides the following key services:
  - **Monitoring**: Real-time data collection from sensors (internal/external temperatures) to track the physical system’s state, enabling detection of deviations from target temperatures.
  - **Optimization**: Calibration algorithms update models of the **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** and its environment to refine parameter estimates, improving control performance and robustness.
  - **Prediction**: Simulations replicate the PT’s behavior under current conditions, allowing forecasting of temperature trends and potential failures (e.g., overheating/underheating).
  - **Control**: Integration with supervisory controllers (e.g., state estimators, fault detectors) to autonomously adjust the heatbed/fan based on model predictions and sensor data, ensuring safe and optimal operation.
  - **Visualization**: Advanced visualizations (not explicitly detailed in documents but implied by DT’s role in Industry 4.0) to aid engineers in interpreting system behavior, debugging, and decision-making.
  Target users include **engineers, operators, and maintenance personnel**, with capabilities supporting **predictive maintenance, adaptive control, and safety compliance**.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The Digital Twin implementation for the incubator relies on the following tools and technologies:
  - **Hardware Platform**: Raspberry Pi as the central control unit, interfacing with sensors (temperature sensors) and actuators (heatbed/fan).
  - **Modeling Frameworks**: Two distinct models are built with **calibration methods** (not specified further) to represent the CPS and its environment, enabling accurate simulations of temperature dynamics.
  - **Simulation Engines**: Implied use of **differential equation-based models** (e.g., for dynamic analysis) to simulate the system’s behavior under varying conditions (Document 1).
  - **Data Collection**: Sensors provide real-time data streams (temperature readings) fed into the DT for monitoring and calibration.
  - **Algorithmic Enablers**: Techniques include **state estimators, fault detectors, and optimizers** (Document 1) to process data/model outputs for decision-making.
  - **Public Repository**: The **INTO-CPS-Association example-incubator repository** ([https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)) serves as a reference for hardware/software specifications, enabling reproducibility and technology adoption.
  - **Calibration Methodologies**: The DT employs **calibration algorithms** to update models dynamically with sensor data, ensuring alignment between the DT and PT (Document 1).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale:
The Digital Twin (DT) for the incubator case study synchronizes data collection and model updates with the physical twin (PT) using **real-time calibration algorithms** (as depicted in fig. 1). The system relies on **sensors** (two internal and one external temperature sensors) to collect data at **continuous or near-continuous intervals**, likely aligned with the **operating cycle of the heatbed and fan** (e.g., every few seconds to maintain temperature stability). The DT’s temporal granularity is optimized for **predictive monitoring and adaptive control**, ensuring minimal latency between physical and digital state updates. The **latency requirements** are implicitly tied to the incubator’s operational demands—maintaining precise temperature regulation within the styrofoam box—suggesting sub-second response times for critical adjustments. The **time scales for services** include:
  - **Data acquisition**: Continuous or high-frequency sampling (e.g., 1–10 Hz).
  - **Model calibration**: Periodic updates (e.g., every few minutes) to refine parameters based on real-time sensor data.
  - **Monitoring/optimization**: Real-time checks for deviations (e.g., via state estimators) and reactive adjustments (e.g., controller actions).
  No explicit frequency or latency metrics are provided, but the DT’s structure implies **low-latency, closed-loop control** to ensure robustness against environmental perturbations (e.g., varying internal content heat generation).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities:
The Digital Twin for the incubator system operates as a **single centralized instance** (no mention of decentralized or distributed twins). The architecture is **monolithic**, with the DT serving as a unified framework for the entire incubator plant (styrofoam box, heatbed, fan, and sensors). There is **no explicit mention of multiple DT instances** or hierarchical coordination between them. The **scope of responsibilities** is confined to the incubator’s physical components, with the DT integrating:
  - **Calibration algorithms** (updating models via sensor data).
  - **Monitors** (safety/optimality checks).
  - **Controllers** (adaptive adjustments to the physical system).
  Coordination mechanisms are implicit: the DT’s **cyber-physical loop** (fig. 1) ensures real-time alignment between the PT and DT, with no external twin dependencies. The **hardware/software setup** (Raspberry Pi + sensors) is tightly coupled, reinforcing a **single-point-of-control** approach.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin constellation for the incubator follows a **closed-loop, cyber-physical architecture** (fig. 1), integrating:
  - **Core components**:
    - **Data**: Collected via sensors (two internal + one external temperature sensors) and transmitted to the DT via a **cyber layer** (likely over Ethernet or Wi-Fi, given the Raspberry Pi’s role).
    - **Models**: Representing the physical system’s dynamics (e.g., heat transfer, fan airflow) and environment (insulated styrofoam box). Models are calibrated using **real-time sensor data** to refine parameters (e.g., thermal resistance, heatbed efficiency).
    - **Algorithms**: Including **state estimators** (to infer hidden states), **fault detectors** (e.g., sensor drift), and **supervisory controllers** (adjusting heatbed/fan based on DT predictions).
  - **Orchestration**:
    The DT employs a **feedback-driven workflow**:
      1. **Data acquisition** → 2. **Model calibration** (via iterative updates) → 3. **Monitoring** (safety/optimality checks) → 4. **Decision-making** (human/algorithm-triggered adjustments to the PT).
    The **architecture pattern** is **centralized**, with the DT acting as the **single source of truth** for the incubator’s state. **Resource management** is implicit: the Raspberry Pi handles real-time processing, while the DT’s models are optimized for **low-latency inference** (e.g., differential equation-based simulations). No explicit orchestration tools (e.g., Kubernetes, Zeppelin) are mentioned, but the **CPS-based structure** (fig. 1) suggests a **hardware-software co-design** approach.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) for the thermal incubator system integrates **two simulation models** for temperature prediction:
1. A **basic model** with **two free parameters** (simpler, less accurate for thermal inertia).
2. A **comprehensive model** with **four free parameters**, which better captures the **thermal inertia of the heatbed** and is preferred for further DT development.

**Model Types**:
- **Behavioral models** (simulation-based) for temperature dynamics, parameterized by physical properties (e.g., heatbed thermal characteristics).
- **Analytical models** (implicit in differential equation formulations, though not explicitly detailed in the documents).

**Data Sources**:
- **Hardware sensors** (implied via the incubator’s insulated box with a heatbed and controller).
- **Simulation data** (generated by the DT’s internal models for validation/testing).

**Data Management Approaches**:
- **Open-source hardware/software platform** (available for development/verification of DT techniques).
- **Calibration algorithms** (used to update models with real-time sensor data for improved parameter estimation, as referenced in the general DT framework).

**Model Relationships**:
- The DT **calibrates models dynamically** using sensor data (e.g., temperature measurements) to adjust parameters (e.g., thermal inertia) in real-time.
- The **four-parameter model** is hierarchically superior to the two-parameter model, enabling more accurate predictions of the Physical Twin (PT) behavior under varying conditions.

**Roles**:
- **Models**: Encode physical laws (e.g., heat transfer) and cyber-physical interactions (e.g., controller dynamics).
- **Data**: Provides real-time feedback for model calibration, validation, and anomaly detection.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The thermal incubator’s **Digital Twin** relies on:
- **Temperature sensors** (implied by the focus on thermal dynamics and model validation for temperature prediction).
- **Spatial distribution**: Sensors likely distributed across the heatbed and incubator interior to capture **thermal gradients** (though exact placement is unspecified).
- **Sampling frequency**: Not explicitly stated, but implied to be **high-frequency** (e.g., seconds or milliseconds) to resolve thermal inertia effects.
- **Data transmission**: Likely via **networked control interfaces** (e.g., embedded computers/communication protocols like Controller Area Network (CAN) in CPS contexts).
- **Accuracy/precision**: Not quantified, but the **four-parameter model** suggests high fidelity is prioritized for thermal measurements (e.g., ±0.1°C or better, inferred from model complexity).
- **Data collection mechanisms**: Real-time logging of sensor data for model calibration and validation (e.g., via software systems for communication and control).
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
**Model Fidelity Requirements**:
- The DT prioritizes the **four-parameter model** over the two-parameter model due to its superior ability to capture **thermal inertia** of the heatbed, ensuring higher fidelity in temperature predictions.

**Validation/Verification Methods**:
- **Experimental validation**: The incubator case study uses **real-world experiments** to compare model predictions (four-parameter vs. two-parameter) against actual temperature measurements.
- **Parameter tuning**: The **calibration algorithms** (implied in the general DT framework) adjust model parameters dynamically using sensor data to minimize prediction errors.

**Uncertainty Sources**:
- **Model assumptions**: Simplifications in the two-parameter model (e.g., neglecting heatbed-specific thermal properties).
- **Sensor noise**: Potential inaccuracies in temperature measurements (though not quantified in the documents).

**Error Detection/Correction**:
- **Monitoring systems** (general DT framework) detect deviations between predicted and actual temperatures, triggering recalibration or alerts.
- **Human/algorithm intervention**: Violations of safety/optimality thresholds (e.g., temperature thresholds) prompt physical adjustments to the incubator’s environment.

**Quality Assurance Processes**:
- **Open-source accessibility**: Ensures reproducibility and community validation of DT techniques.
- **Model hierarchy**: The four-parameter model is **preferred for further development**, implying rigorous validation criteria (e.g., root-mean-square error (RMSE) thresholds).
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in the incubator Digital Twin (DT) involves **real-time sensor data collection** from the Physical Twin (PT) via the following mechanisms:
  - **Data Sources**: Temperature sensors (internal and external) monitor the incubator's environment, transmitting measurements at unspecified intervals (implied by operational requirements).
  - **Data Types**: Raw temperature readings (likely in Celsius or Kelvin) are transmitted, with no explicit mention of additional metadata (e.g., timestamps, sensor IDs, or environmental context).
  - **Communication Protocols**: Not explicitly detailed, but implied to use **CPS (Cyber-Physical System) protocols** (e.g., CAN, Modbus, or custom IoT protocols) for sensor-to-DT data transfer, given the Raspberry Pi’s role as the control hub.
  - **Event Triggers**: Data transmission is likely **periodic** (e.g., every second or minute) or **event-driven** (e.g., triggered by temperature thresholds or sensor failures).
  - **Data Quality Assurance**: Calibration algorithms (referenced in Document 1) are used to update models with the best parameter estimates, ensuring data accuracy. No explicit mention of filtering, noise reduction, or redundancy checks.
  - **Preprocessing Steps**: Implied via the DT’s calibration algorithms (Document 1), which integrate sensor data into cyber-physical models for validation and optimization.
  - **Data Integration Mechanisms**: Sensors feed into the DT’s **model-based framework** (e.g., differential equations for dynamics), enabling real-time synchronization between physical and virtual states.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in the incubator DT involves **command execution and feedback loops** via the Raspberry Pi controller:
  - **Command Types**: Control commands include **temperature setpoints** (e.g., heating/fan adjustments) and **reconfiguration directives** (e.g., adjusting insulation or sensor placements).
  - **Control Message Formats**: Not explicitly detailed, but likely structured as **binary/ASCII commands** (e.g., PWM signals for the heatbed, digital signals for the fan) via the same CPS protocols used for sensor data.
  - **Validation/Authentication**: No explicit mention of cryptographic validation (e.g., TLS, digital signatures), but the DT’s **calibration algorithms** (Document 1) may implicitly enforce consistency checks between virtual commands and physical responses.
  - **Feedback Systems**: The DT monitors **temperature deviations** (via sensors) and triggers corrections (e.g., adjusting heatbed power or fan speed) to maintain target conditions.
  - **Conflict Resolution**: Implied by the DT’s **supervisory control** (Document 1), where algorithms prioritize safety/optimality over immediate physical commands (e.g., overriding manual overrides if a threshold is violated).
  - **Command Execution Confirmation**: Not explicitly documented, but the DT’s **state estimators** (Document 1) likely verify alignment between virtual commands and physical outcomes via sensor feedback.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **technical network infrastructure** for the incubator DT relies on:
  - **Communication Protocols**: Likely **custom IoT protocols** or **CPS-specific protocols** (e.g., CAN, Modbus, or Ethernet-based) for sensor-to-DT and DT-to-Raspberry Pi communication.
  - **Network Topology**: **Star topology** (Raspberry Pi as central hub) or **mesh topology** (if sensors share data via a network), given the incubator’s constrained environment.
  - **Bandwidth/Latency Requirements**: Not specified, but **real-time criticality** is implied for temperature regulation (e.g., <100ms latency for control loops).
  - **Reliability/Redundancy**: No explicit redundancy mechanisms, but the DT’s **calibration algorithms** (Document 1) may handle transient data loss by recalibrating models dynamically.
  - **Security Protocols**: Not mentioned; assumed minimal (e.g., basic firewall rules) due to the incubator’s controlled environment, but **DT integration** (Document 1) suggests potential future expansion to include **IoT security frameworks** (e.g., MQTT over TLS).
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **deployment infrastructure** for the incubator DT is **on-premise/edge-based**:
  - **Hosting Platforms**: Deployed on a **Raspberry Pi** (embedded edge device) hosting the DT’s core algorithms (e.g., calibration, state estimation).
  - **Computational Requirements**: Low-power processing (Raspberry Pi’s CPU/GPU) for real-time simulations (e.g., differential equation solvers) and lightweight algorithms (e.g., fault detection).
  - **Storage Requirements**: Minimal (sensor logs, model parameters) stored locally or in cloud-edge sync (if applicable), with no explicit mention of persistent storage.
  - **Scalability Mechanisms**: Not detailed, but the DT’s **modular architecture** (Document 1) allows incremental addition of sensors/models without redeployment.
  - **Deployment Models**: **Single-instance deployment** (Raspberry Pi as sole DT host) with potential for **cloud-edge hybrid** scaling if extended to additional incubators.
  - **Availability/Reliability Assurance**: Implied by the DT’s **calibration-driven resilience** (Document 1), where model updates mitigate hardware failures (e.g., sensor drift).
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
**Lifecycle Phases of the Digital Twin (DT) for the Incubator Case Study:**
  The lifecycle of the DT for the incubator system, as implied by the documents, follows a structured yet flexible evolution tied to the physical twin (PT) lifecycle. While the documents do not explicitly enumerate formal stages, the **development and operational phases** can be inferred as follows:

  1. **Design and Modeling Phase**:
     - **Representation**: Models are built at varying abstraction levels (e.g., CAD-like structural models for geometry, dynamic models via differential equations for thermal dynamics). The DT integrates **calibration algorithms** (e.g., parameter estimation) to refine models against real-time sensor data (e.g., temperature sensors in the PT).
     - **Transition Management**: The DT’s models are continuously updated via **calibration loops** (e.g., using data from the PT’s sensors) to align with operational conditions, ensuring consistency between cyber and physical domains.

  2. **Deployment and Runtime Phase**:
     - **Representation**: The DT operates in real-time, leveraging **data streams** (collected via sensors) and **algorithms** (e.g., fault detectors, state estimators) to monitor and optimize the PT’s performance (e.g., temperature regulation in the incubator).
     - **Lifecycle Governance**: The DT’s evolution is governed by **continuous feedback loops** between the PT and DT, where violations (e.g., temperature deviations) trigger interventions (e.g., adjustments via Raspberry Pi controllers). The **Incubator’s hardware/software specifications** (accessible via the public repository) serve as a baseline for governance, ensuring compatibility and adaptability to environmental changes (e.g., varying contents inside the PT).

  **Key Note**: The documents emphasize that models are **not discarded post-deployment** but integrated into the DT for ongoing optimization and safety monitoring. The lifecycle governance relies on **calibration-driven updates** and **real-time monitoring**, with no explicit mention of formal lifecycle frameworks (e.g., ISO 14204) or version control systems beyond the DT’s iterative calibration process.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
**Engineering and Development Process for the Incubator Digital Twin:**
  The twinning process for the incubator DT is grounded in a **hybrid cyber-physical system (CPS) approach**, with evolution managed through iterative calibration and sensor-driven feedback. Key methodologies and practices include:

  1. **Methodologies**:
     - **Modeling**: Two distinct models are built (as per Section 3), likely including:
       - A **structural model** (e.g., CAD-inspired for the styrofoam box, heatbed, and fan geometry).
       - A **dynamic model** (e.g., differential equations for thermal regulation, calibrated via sensor data).
     - **Calibration**: Algorithms (e.g., parameter estimation) update models in real-time using data from the PT’s **two internal temperature sensors** and **one external sensor**, ensuring alignment between cyber and physical domains.

  2. **Quality Assurance and Testing**:
     - **Validation**: Experimental results (Section 4) demonstrate the DT’s ability to regulate temperature within the PT, validating its accuracy and robustness. The **Incubator’s hardware/software repository** provides a reference for testing configurations.
     - **Version Control**: While not explicitly detailed, the DT’s evolution implies **incremental updates** tied to sensor data streams and calibration iterations, with no mention of formal versioning systems (e.g., Git for DT models).

  3. **Evolution Management**:
     - The DT’s evolution is **environmentally adaptive**: It reconfigures (e.g., via Raspberry Pi) to handle changes in the PT’s contents (e.g., varying thermal loads). The **calibration loop** (Fig. 1) ensures continuous adaptation without manual intervention, though human oversight may intervene for critical violations.
     - **Future Work**: Section 5 hints at expanding the DT’s goals (e.g., robustness, safety) via additional techniques, suggesting iterative refinement without rigid lifecycle stages.

  **Technologies/Tools**:
     - **Hardware**: Raspberry Pi (control platform), sensors (temperature), styrofoam box (insulation).
     - **Software**: Calibration algorithms (parameter estimation), simulation tools (differential equations), and monitoring frameworks (e.g., for fault detection).
     - **Public Repository**: [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator) provides hardware/software specs for reproducibility.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
**Decision Support Capabilities of the Digital Twin:**
  The incubator DT generates insights and enables decision-making through **real-time data analytics and algorithmic interventions**, with the following capabilities:

  1. **Insights Generated**:
     - **Thermal Dynamics**: Predicts and monitors the PT’s temperature profile using dynamic models calibrated via sensor data (e.g., internal/external temperatures).
     - **Fault Detection**: Algorithms (e.g., state estimators) identify deviations (e.g., sensor malfunctions, insulation inefficiencies) via anomaly detection in temperature readings.
     - **Optimization**: Monitors the PT’s performance (e.g., heating efficiency) and suggests adjustments (e.g., fan speed, heatbed power) to achieve target temperatures.

  2. **Analytics and Processing**:
     - **Data Flow**: Sensor data (e.g., 2 internal + 1 external temperature sensors) streams continuously, feeding into:
       - **Model Calibration**: Updates parameters (e.g., thermal conductivity) to improve prediction accuracy.
       - **Monitoring**: Violations (e.g., temperature > target) trigger alerts or automated corrections via the Raspberry Pi.
     - **Algorithms**: Includes:
       - **State Estimators**: Infer hidden states (e.g., internal temperature gradients) from noisy sensor data.
       - **Optimizers**: Adjust PT parameters (e.g., heatbed power) to minimize deviations.

  3. **Decision-Making Processes**:
     - **Autonomous Actions**: The DT autonomously intervenes (e.g., adjusts fan/heatbed) when violations exceed thresholds, with no explicit mention of human-in-the-loop (HITL) decision gates.
     - **Confidence Measures**: The DT’s confidence is implicitly tied to model calibration quality (e.g., reduced error via sensor data alignment). Experimental results (Section 4) validate its predictive accuracy for temperature regulation.

  4. **User Interaction Methods**:
     - **Visualizations**: While not detailed, the DT likely provides dashboards (e.g., temperature graphs) for operators to review performance and intervene if needed.
     - **Alerts**: Critical violations (e.g., overheating) may trigger alerts to human operators or automated controllers.

  **Example Workflow**:
     - A user sets a target temperature. The DT’s dynamic model predicts thermal behavior, while sensors feed real-time data into calibration algorithms. If the PT’s temperature deviates, the DT’s optimizer adjusts the heatbed/fan, and the cycle repeats.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: A Digital Twin (DT) system incorporates **cybersecurity protocols** and **safety-critical mechanisms** to ensure robust protection of both the physical and virtual twins. The system relies on **calibration algorithms** to dynamically update models of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) and their environments, integrating real-time sensor data to enhance accuracy and reliability. **Access control mechanisms** are implicitly implied through the integration of monitoring systems that validate system performance and detect deviations (e.g., violations of safety constraints) via **state estimators** and **supervisory controllers**. While the documents do not explicitly mention explicit data ownership policies, privacy protection measures, or compliance frameworks, the core emphasis lies on **fail-safe mechanisms**—automated responses triggered by detected anomalies (e.g., unsafe operating conditions) to prevent catastrophic failures. The system’s **risk assessment approaches** are implicitly embedded in the calibration and monitoring workflows, where deviations trigger corrective actions (human or algorithmic) to maintain optimal and safe operation.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator


1 It is worth noting that models can be described at different levels of abstraction, and typically there is a
tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.
2 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


2


Figure 1: Schematic overview of a CPS-based Digital Twins. The letter “C” and “P”
represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of

1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1
block_4_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
